_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Urgent surgical exploration of the plexus if a root avulsion or laceration injury to the plexus is suspected.
Urgent surgical exploration of the plexus in cases of open injury, retained foreign body, or vascular injury requiring vessel repair.
Sharp transections of the brachial plexus allowing for primary repair of the transected ends to optimize nerve regeneration.
Delayed exploration indicated at three to six months after injury if the patient fails to regain neurologic function satisfactorily.

preauth.label: Clinical Notes detailing the injury and need for surgery
Medico legal case report/ FIR copy of accident, if the injury was due to accident

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes – details of accident, signs & symptoms, indication for surgery, and planned line of treatment?
Was the Medico legal report/ FIR copy of the accident submitted, if the injury was due to accident?
Was the nerve conductional velocity report/ EMG report/ MRI report submitted?

cpd_claim.q: Were the indoor case papers submitted?
Are the detailed Procedure/ Operation notes submitted?
Is there a Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?

common_queries: Clinical notes not suggestive of nerve plexus injury.
Clinical notes detailing injury and need for surgery missing or inadequate.
Medico legal report or FIR copy missing when injury is due to accident.
Nerve conduction velocity report, EMG report, or MRI report not submitted when indicated.
Indoor case papers missing or incomplete at claim submission.
Procedure/Operation notes missing or lacking detail at claim submission.
Detailed discharge summary not submitted or missing follow-up advice.

clinical_key_pointers: Obtaining a thorough history in a patient with possible NPI is necessary to identify the injury mechanism, associated fractures, and other concomitant trauma to the neck, shoulder, and chest.
Careful inspection of the patient can provide important clues to the extent of the injury.
Considerable bruising and swelling may indicate deep trauma with possible vascular injury, which can be confirmed by evaluating the radial, ulnar, and brachial pulses.
A detailed neurologic examination is warranted to ascertain the location of the lesion.
Findings on initial examination are supplemented with advanced imaging and electrophysiological evaluation to determine the prognosis of neurologic recovery further.
surgical_options: Urgent surgical exploration for root avulsion, laceration injury, open injury, retained foreign body, or vascular injury requiring vessel repair.
Primary repair of sharply transected brachial plexus ends to optimize nerve regeneration.
Conservative treatment for low-energy mechanism Brachial plexus injury with favorable prognosis for spontaneous recovery.
Delayed exploration (3 to 6 months after injury) if the patient fails to regain neurologic function satisfactorily.
additional_information.post_op_care: The patient should be closely followed with serial examinations, imaging studies, and electrophysiological testing.
contraindications: Patient not medically or psychologically cleared for surgery.
Local wounds or infections obstructing the surgical approach.
